item.title: UAE opens up an Opec fissure
item.description: Exit represents a rejection of the production-control system dominated in recent years by Saudi Arabia

item.title: Iran war oil price windfall gives Shell a chance to edge ahead of BP
item.description: Extra funds mean majors can now in theory increase exploration without cutting shareholder returns

item.title: It’s never a good idea to sack the entire National Science Board
item.description: Donald Trump is spurning the research that makes progress possible

item.title: The great commodities disruption
item.description: Trump’s attack on Iran has served as a reminder of how interconnected the global economy remains

item.title: How can we prevent our children from fighting over our estate?
item.description: We worry they will fall out over their inheritance

item.title: The stablecoin stumbling block
item.description: The crypto tokens are not yet safe enough to be used at scale in the settlement of large, wholesale financial transactions

item.title: Rage in the age of X
item.description: It feels like politics is more fraught today — is it?

item.title: Britain’s top furniture designers are made in Milan
item.description: Tom Dixon’s control of his own brand makes him an outlier among London creatives

item.title: Brown-Forman’s choice: straight-up or with an M&A mixer
item.description: Maker of Jack Daniel’s breaks off talks with Pernod, but other value-creating tie-ups may still make sense

item.title: Beijing’s blunt message to its tech sector
item.description: China’s ban on Meta-Manus deal is a warning to keep AI innovations at home

item.title: Spirit Airlines’ dismal prospects could soon be everyone’s problem
item.description: Lenders may flinch at a government bailout but the alternative could be worse

item.title: What the Grasset affair tells us about modern elites
item.description: Protests by authors at the French publishing house are a symptom of the struggle between establishment and insurgents

item.title: Russia’s loss is Ukraine’s gain in the Middle East
item.description: Moscow’s ties with Iran and its waning power in the region give Kyiv a chance to forge new partnerships in the Gulf

item.title: Get used to the long Iran war
item.description: Tehran has a strong incentive to keep the conflict going

item.title: Wall Street is getting twitchy on oil prices
item.description: Plus, dollar dreams and reality

item.title: The real lesson of Reform’s war on the history curriculum
item.description: Proposals for a new patriotic syllabus reveal the ambiguous appeal of rightwing populism

item.title: Iran war shows Latin America has left its original sin behind
item.description: For 30 years, every oil shock broke Latin American bonds. This one didn’t

item.title: Rivian is bringing software-as-a-service to the road
item.description: Electric-vehicle maker wants to license its code for other carmakers

item.title: A troubling plan to revive the frozen US property market
item.description: Buyers should beware the real estate dark pools

item.title: Honda and the power of (broken) dreams
item.description: A brilliant and adventurist beacon for Japanese industry in the past is now stumbling

item.title: King Charles and the ‘special’ relationship
item.description: Ties with the US need mending, but Britain must nurture other alliances too

item.title: Meta’s Chinese stumble suggests declining tolerance for shades of grey
item.description: Tech-related capital flows have benefited from decades of ambiguity, but AI changes the calculus

item.title: Why London’s teens need more third spaces
item.description: At a time when young people are either demonised for staying in or going out, youth clubs are more important than ever

item.title: The EU’s methane regulation could spark an energy crisis
item.description: Its extraterritorial, prescriptive and paperwork-heavy requirements will restrict access to supply options

item.title: Why flying less won’t do much to ease the oil crisis
item.description: Crude demand weakens when not only jet fuel but other product prices rise too
